terms.definition: Electrical potential difference that drives current through a circuit.
The flow rate of electric charge, measured in amperes.
Opposition to current flow, measured in ohms.
Central Processing Unit; executes instructions for the device.
Random Access Memory; temporary fast storage for active tasks.
Long-term memory (eMMC/NVMe) that keeps your files, apps, and photos even when powered off.
Graphics Processing Unit; renders images, video, and game graphics.
Low-level software that controls hardware directly.
Program that loads the operating system when a device starts.
Physical/logical interface connecting a chip to the motherboard.
Reducing performance to control excess heat.
Component that sends/receives wireless signals.
How many cycles per second a CPU core can perform, measured in GHz.
An independent processing unit inside a CPU; more cores allow more tasks in parallel.
Very fast memory inside the CPU that stores frequently used data.
The chemical composition (e.g. Li-ion, Li-Po) that determines a battery's capacity, safety, and lifespan.
Milliamp-hour; a unit measuring how much charge a battery can store.
Technology that delivers higher wattage to charge a battery more quickly, at the cost of extra heat.
How many times per second a display redraws the image, measured in Hz. Higher feels smoother.
The number of pixels on a display; higher resolution shows sharper detail.
A display technology using organic LEDs for deep blacks and vivid colors, with more flexibility than LCD.
Liquid Crystal Display; an older, more affordable, energy-efficient display technology.
A unit describing camera resolution; more megapixels can capture more image detail.
A specialized processor that speeds up tasks like image recognition, translation, and computational photography.
A component that detects a physical property (motion, light, pressure) and converts it to data.
A sensor that detects motion and acceleration, used for step counting and screen rotation.
A sensor that detects rotation and orientation, used in gaming and camera stabilization.
A sensor measuring air pressure, used to estimate altitude.
Near Field Communication; enables tap-to-pay and quick device pairing over very short range.
Global Positioning System; uses satellites to determine your device's precise location.
A wireless technology for connecting to the internet over short-to-medium range.
A short-range wireless technology used for connecting headphones, watches, and accessories.
The main circuit board that connects and coordinates every other component in a device.
A group of chips on the motherboard that manages data flow between CPU, memory, and peripherals.
A chip that regulates and distributes correct voltage levels to every component.
A chip that controls how fast and safely a battery charges.
The process of removing excess heat from components to prevent damage or slowdown.
A cooling technology that spreads heat across a wider surface using evaporating liquid.
The core software that manages hardware and lets you run apps.
Software that allows the operating system to communicate with a specific piece of hardware.
The maximum rate of data transfer across a connection, often measured in Mbps or Gbps.
The delay between an action and the system's response, important for gaming and calls.
Running a component faster than its default speed to boost performance, usually increasing heat.
Whether two components can work correctly together based on matching specifications.
A measure of how well a device can send and receive wireless signals.
The layer under a touchscreen that detects finger touches and converts them to input signals.
A standard rating showing how resistant a device is to dust and water.
A single chip that integrates CPU, GPU, and other controllers together for efficiency.
A flexible screen that can bend or fold, used in foldable phone designs.
An ultra low-power display technology that only uses energy when the image changes, common in e-readers.
A newer battery chemistry using solid electrolytes, offering higher capacity with less heat than Li-Po.
A camera design using an internal mirror system to achieve high zoom levels in a slim phone body.
A front camera hidden beneath the screen itself, removing the need for a notch or hole-punch cutout.
Universal Flash Storage; a fast storage standard commonly used in mid-range and flagship phones.
The newest WiFi standard offering very high speed, lower latency, and better performance in crowded networks.
A modern Bluetooth audio standard offering better battery efficiency and support for multiple connected listeners.
A GPS technique that uses network data to lock onto your location faster than GPS satellites alone.
A surround sound audio technology that creates a immersive, 3D-like listening experience.
An individual regulated voltage output line from a Power Management IC, dedicated to a specific component.
An optical sensor that measures pulse by detecting blood flow changes, common in fitness tracking.
A sensor that measures ultraviolet light intensity, useful for sun exposure warnings.
A depth sensor that measures distance by timing how long light takes to bounce back, used for portrait blur and face unlock.
A small chip that identifies your phone number and connects you to a cellular network.
A phone feature allowing two SIM cards to be used at once, for separate numbers or networks.
An embedded SIM built directly into the device, activated digitally without needing a physical card.
A small motor that creates physical buzzing feedback for notifications, typing, or gaming.
Precise, tactile vibration feedback designed to feel crisp and intentional, rather than a generic buzz.
A bundle of software drivers that lets the operating system properly communicate with installed hardware.
A customizable physical button that can be programmed to trigger a chosen shortcut or app.
The ability of a device to run and switch between several apps at once without slowing down.
A camera lens capable of magnifying distant subjects, often measured in optical zoom (e.g. 5x, 10x).
The percentage of a phone's front surface occupied by the display, versus bezels and notches.
